summary: DHH uses the iconic 'cornflower blue' scene from Fight Club as an extended metaphor to eviscerate Apple's class-action settlement with developers. He argues the settlement is a cynical performance where lawyers extracted $30M in fees while securing only meaningless concessions that reaffirm existing Apple policies. The supposed win of letting developers email customers about alternative payment methods is exposed as a 'clarification' of what was already happening, while the actual App Store steering restrictions remain intact. DHH contends this corrupt deal actively harms developers by cementing Apple's monopoly power under the guise of legal advocacy.
key_points: The settlement's headline concessions merely reaffirm existing Apple policies, like search ranking based on objective characteristics, with a three-year expiration
The supposed victory of allowing developers to contact customers about outside purchasing is just a 'clarification' about opt-in emails, not actual in-app communication
Class-action lawyers walked away with $30M in fees from an Apple-administered fund while delivering zero meaningful policy changes for developers
The settlement actually strengthens Apple's position by drawing thicker lines around its App Store steering restrictions and asserting its right to control in-app purchasing
The outcome is worse than no lawsuit at all, as it demonstrates that even class-action suits can be neutralized with bromides and bribes
money_quotes: The only human response to this exercise of soul-sucking drudgery is to show your teeth soaked in blood.
Even when a class-action lawsuit gets underway, it can be bought with bromides and bribes.
The point of the negotiation was never to extract any meaningful change of policy or behavior, but to provide cover for the process.

Developers have suffered a litany of indignities under Apple's monopoly power over the years, and now they'll suffer a few more. The twist being that they now come from the hands of a group of legal vultures pretending to advocate on developer's behalf but are really just paid to collude with Apple.
Far worse than if no suit has been undertaken at all. If anything, this settlement cements the tremendous power that Apple has and wields.
It's a cynical dance where both parties know that the other side is both acting and full of shit.
I am Jack's complete lack of surprise.
key_insight: Apple's class-action settlement is a theatrical performance where lawyers enriched themselves by $30M while cementing the very monopoly power they claimed to challenge, leaving developers worse off than before.
